IPMI wakeup means the IPMI sends a command to the BMC on the host to be woken up
Network wakeup and IPMI wakeup are the two host wakeup methods supported
Serial console BREAK signaling and PXE remote boot are the standard DPM wakeup methods on H3C CAS
explanation: The PDF host wakeup methods page lists only Network wakeup and IPMI wakeup; serial console BREAK and PXE remote boot are not listed.

question: Among the following descriptions of the H3C CAS USB network redirection technology, which is incorrect?
options: The virtual USB driver in the operating system of the VM initiates a control or read/write request to a physical USB
A TCP network connection is established between the USB virtual interface of the QEMU simulation layer and the USB redirection service on the host
The USB redirection service on the host forwards the USB operation request to the physical USB located on the host, and the physical USB responds back through the network
USB redirection moves the physical USB controller via Intel VT-d PCIe passthrough to the destination CVK during VM migration
explanation: The PDF USB redirection page describes a TCP/network-based USB redirection protocol with a software USB virtual interface; it is not VT-d PCIe passthrough of the USB controller.

question: Among the following statements about the H3C CAS linked clone VM cache optimization, which is wrong?
options: In a linked clone scenario, the VM may read content of a base image
In the case of batch startup, the reading pressure of the base image will be very large, so that the batch startup speed in the linked clone scenario is very slow
Adding a cache of the base image on each CVK lets the content of the base image be read only once when VMs are started in batches
The linked clone cache optimization works by replicating the entire base image to each VM's dedicated disk so the base image is no longer shared
explanation: The PDF linked clone page states the optimization is adding a base-image cache on each CVK so the base image is read only once during batch startup; the base image remains shared, not replicated per VM.

question: Regarding the H3C CAS asynchronous remote replication used in SRM homogeneous disaster recovery, which of the following statements are incorrect?
options: The asynchronous replication is generally performed periodically and the consistency of data between data volumes cannot be guaranteed
Asynchronous replication releases each I/O write only after both the master volume and the remote volume return that the write operations are completed
If a disaster occurs, data that has not been updated on the remote volume has the risk of being lost, that is, a time window
Asynchronous replication has relatively low requirements on the bandwidth and distance
Asynchronous replication has a higher level of data integrity than synchronous replication because it batches writes
explanation: Option 1 is the synchronous replication property; asynchronous releases an I/O write after the local master volume completes the write and replicates a snapshot. Option 4 reverses the integrity ordering: synchronous has the highest data integrity, not asynchronous.

question: Regarding the H3C CAS DPM policy prerequisites, which of the following are incorrect?
options: All hosts in the cluster must use the same shared storage
VMs in the cluster must use the same shared storage
VMs in the cluster must enable the Auto Migration function
Hosts in the cluster must enable the Wakeup function
All VMs in the cluster must have SR-IOV VF passthrough enabled so DPM can preserve network performance after migration
explanation: The PDF DPM policy page lists exactly the four prerequisites (same shared storage on hosts, same shared storage on VMs, Auto Migration on VMs, Wakeup on hosts); SR-IOV VF passthrough is not a DPM prerequisite.

question: Among the following descriptions of H3C disaster recovery deployment types and All-active solution, which are wrong?
options: In an All-active solution one service is provided to the outside by multiple centers simultaneously and multiple data centers can carry service pressure shared proportionally
After one center stops serving, service traffic can be automatically switched to another center, and the automatic switching is transparent to the access side
A dual data center deployment can be based on service sharing modes including active-standby, operation-operation, and active-active
A single data center deployment alone fully resists central disasters and removes the need for cross-center replication
A three-centers-in-two-places deployment includes two local centers plus one remote standby center
explanation: The PDF deployment-types page explicitly says a single data center 'Resist[s] the overall failure of the device' but only dual/multi data centers 'Resist central disasters'; option 3 contradicts this.

question: Regarding the H3C CAS Heterogeneous CDP disaster recovery solution, which of the following statements are incorrect?
options: CDP is not limited to the form of the production DC; it can protect CAS or other production environments
CDP supports protection of bare-metal and virtualization workloads from heterogeneous platforms such as HPE, Dell, VMware, Hyper-V, Nutanix and Sangfor
CDP achieves RPO of about 1 minute and minute-level RTO
CDP eliminates the need for any agent on the production-side workloads because it uses array-based storage replication
CDP requires storage-array-based SRA plug-ins provided by storage manufacturers to perform the production-to-DR switchover
explanation: The PDF CDP page shows Agent-based protection of critical apps on bare metal and heterogeneous virtualization, contradicting option 3. SRA plug-ins are the SRM homogeneous-DR mechanism, not CDP, contradicting option 4.

question: Which of the following commands is used to query the VM disk structure in the background of a CVK host?
options: virsh pool-list --all
virsh domblklist ID
virsh list --all
qemu-img info <VM image file>
explanation: Per the PDF, 'virsh domblklist ID' queries the VM disk structure in the background of the CVK host. 'virsh pool-list --all' queries storage pool operation, 'virsh list --all' queries VMs, and 'qemu-img info' queries image file information.

question: Which of the following commands is used to query storage pool operation in the background of a CVK host?
options: virsh pool-status
virsh pool-list --all
virsh storage-list
ovs-vsctl pool-show
explanation: The PDF documents 'virsh pool-list --all' as the command to query storage pool operation in the background of the CVK host, returning Name/State/Autostart columns for defaultpool, isopool, ONEStor_High_LUN01, etc.

question: Which of the following commands is used to restart the libvirtd service on a CVK host?
options: service libvirt-restart
systemctl libvirtd reload
service libvirtd restart
virsh libvirtd-restart
explanation: The PDF documents 'service libvirtd restart' as the casserver service start command set used to restart the libvirtd daemon on the CVK host.

question: Which qemu command is used to combine VM image files into a single qcow2 file?
options: qemu-img info <VM image file>
qemu-img merge -f qcow2 xxx
qemu-img convert -O qcow2 -f qcow2 xxx
qemu-img combine -O qcow2 xxx
explanation: The PDF documents 'qemu-img convert -O qcow2 -f qcow2 xxx' to combine image files. 'qemu-img info' queries image information, and the other forms are not documented.

question: Which Linux command listed in the CAS common maintenance commands is used to capture packets on a CVK host?
options: ifconfig
tcpdump
iostat
uptime
explanation: The PDF lists 'tcpdump: Capture packets' among the common Linux commands. 'ifconfig' queries NIC information, 'iostat' queries device load, and 'uptime' queries system start time and load.

question: According to the PDF, which key on the keyboard is used in vi/vim to switch back from edit mode to the common mode?
options: Ctrl+C
Tab
ESC
:q
explanation: The PDF states 'Press "ESC" to switch back to the common mode' in the vi/vim workflow. ':wq' is used to save and exit; ':q' alone is not listed; Ctrl+C and Tab are not documented mode-switch keys for vi in the source.

question: In the iostat output described in the CAS maintenance commands, what does the column header 'tps' represent?
options: Total physical sectors written by the device.
The number of IO read and write requests issued by the process per second.
The number of TCP packets sent per second.
Total partitions per disk device.
explanation: The PDF iostat field table defines tps as 'The number of IO read and write requests issued by the process per second'. The other definitions belong to kB_read/kB_wrtn or are not iostat metrics.

question: Which of the following Linux commands is documented in the CAS maintenance commands for copying files remotely between hosts?
options: cp
scp
mv
tar
explanation: The PDF lists 'scp: Copy files remotely'. 'cp' is local copy, 'mv' moves/renames files, and 'tar' is used to compress and pack files.

question: Which of the following are valid options listed in the upgrade.sh script usage for CAS version upgrade?
options: -p cvks
precheck
postcheck
--force-cvk
--rollback-cvm
explanation: The PDF shows: Usage: ./upgrade.sh [option]  option: [ | -p cvks | precheck | postcheck | copy | -f cvm | -f cvk | --force-cvk | restore | mysql]. '--rollback-cvm' is not in the documented option list.

question: According to the PDF, which steps are part of the documented procedure for replacing the network interface card (NIC) on a CVK host?
options: Back up NIC configuration files
Migrate VMs and back up management platform data
Enter maintenance mode and shut down the host
After replacement, check the cluster status and perform one-key inspection
Skip the cluster-health pre-check to accelerate the swap
explanation: The PDF's NIC replacement workflow includes check cluster health, back up NIC configuration files, migrate VMs, back up management platform data, enter maintenance mode, shut down the host, replace the NIC, exit maintenance mode, check cluster status, and one-key inspection.

question: Which of the following statements about NIC replacement and the resulting configuration files on a CVK host are correct?
options: On Ubuntu, the NIC configuration file is /etc/udev/rules.d/70-persistent-net.rules.
On CentOS, the NIC configuration file is located at /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-ethx.
After a NIC replacement on Ubuntu, the eth number of the new NIC is appended after the original entry, and the MAC must be updated to restore the network.
On CentOS, no new ifcfg file is created after a NIC replacement; only the existing file's MAC is rewritten automatically.
Backing up NIC configuration files is unnecessary before NIC replacement on either OS.
explanation: The PDF specifies Ubuntu path /etc/udev/rules.d/70-persistent-net.rules and CentOS path /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-ethx, and that after replacement the new eth number follows the original and the MAC address must be updated. The PDF also states a NEW ifcfg-ethx file IS added in CentOS, and NIC config files MUST be backed up.

question: Which of the following statements about the stateful-failover (CVM HA) cmsd service procedures described in the PDF are correct?
options: Modifying the virtual IP address requires stopping the cmsd service, modifying the IP, and starting the cmsd service.
When adding a new CVK node as an advanced host, copy the /etc/cmsd folder from the existing node to the new node.
On Ubuntu, 'service cron restart' is run after appending the ms_cmsd_realtime_check.sh crontab entry.
On H3Linux, the cmsd service is enabled with 'systemctl enable cmsd.service' so it starts automatically.
Modifying the virtual IP address requires destroying and reinstalling the entire cmsd configuration package.
explanation: The PDF documents the Modify Virtual IP procedure (Check status, Stop cmsd, Check, Modify IP, Start cmsd, Check), Copy /etc/cmsd to the new CVK node, 'service cron restart' on Ubuntu after the crontab edit, and 'systemctl enable cmsd.service' on H3Linux. A destructive reinstall is not described.

question: Which of the following items are listed in the CAS Performance Optimization configuration ideas?
options: Configure I/O Priority
Configure CPU Model
Configure HugePages (enable host first, then enable at VM)
Disk Provision choose Lazy Zeroed
Enable Memory Ballooning by default on every VM
explanation: The PDF numbers four optimization items: 1) Configure I/O Priority, 2) Configure CPU Model, 3) Configure HugePages (enable host first, then VM), 4) Disk Provision choose 'Lazy Zeroed'. Memory Ballooning is not part of the optimization list.

question: Which of the following resolution steps are documented for a VM console that does not respond to keyboard input on a failed Windows VM (after Ctrl+Alt+Del)?
options: If the Windows disk is corrupt, mount an .iso repair image file onto the VM.
Configure the VM to start from the CD/DVD drive, then restart the VM and open its console.
Press any key at the boot prompt in the console to start from CD/DVD for reparation or reinstallation.
If no guest OS is installed, mount the guest OS .iso or configure the VM to obtain the guest OS from a remote server over the network.
Force-delete the VM and recreate it from scratch with a different hypervisor.
explanation: The PDF lists the .iso repair mount, CD/DVD boot order, press-any-key prompt, and remote/network OS install as the official steps. There is no recommendation to delete the VM or switch hypervisors.

question: Regarding the documented qemu and virsh commands on a CVK host, which of the following statements is INCORRECT?
options: 'qemu-img info <VM image file>' queries VM image file information.
'virsh domblklist ID' queries the VM disk structure in the background of the CVK host.
'virsh pool-list --all' queries storage pool operation in the background.
'virsh image-merge' is the documented command to combine multiple qcow2 image files into one.
explanation: The PDF documents 'qemu-img convert -O qcow2 -f qcow2 xxx' (not 'virsh image-merge') to combine image files. 'virsh image-merge' is not a documented command.

question: Concerning the troubleshooting answer for a garbled CVM webpage when logging in to CVM, which of the following statements is INCORRECT?
options: Accessing one system from multiple browser windows on one computer can cause data exceptions.
Resizing browser windows several times in a short interval can cause garbled CVM webpages.
A browser containing buffered webpage contents is a documented condition leading to the garbled webpage.
The recommended resolution is to open multiple tabs and use a different browser for each, rather than clearing the buffer.
explanation: The PDF's resolution is exactly the opposite: open only one tab in only one browser window on a computer to access CVM, and clear the buffer of the browser before logging in again. Multiple tabs/browsers are documented causes of the issue, not the fix.

question: Regarding the procedure to modify the IP address and host name of a CVK host described in the PDF, which of the following descriptions is INCORRECT?
options: Migrate the VMs off the host before modifying.
Delete the host from the cluster, then modify the IP / host name.
Add the host again, then migrate the VMs back to the host.
It is sufficient to edit the host file via SSH while the host remains in the cluster, without migrating any VMs.
explanation: The PDF process is: Migrate the VM, delete the host from the cluster, modify the IP/host name, add the host again, migrate the VM back. Editing in-place while keeping the host in the cluster is not the documented procedure.

question: Regarding the documented troubleshooting for the 'Disabling IRQ #10 BUG: Soft lockup - CPU#0 stuck for 67s! [migration/0:5]' error when shutting down Linux VMs on CAS, which statements are INCORRECT? (Select all that apply.)
options: The error typically occurs when a VM uses a virtio NIC with poor compatibility with some Linux versions.
When this error message appears, the VM shutdown procedure is almost finished, and the VM can be powered off from CAS to force shutdown without incurring problems.
The recommended solution is to immediately downgrade the host's libvirtd to a previous version and reboot the entire cluster.
The recommended solution is to replace the virtio NIC with an e1000 NIC in production before any attempt to power off the VM.
Power-off from CAS is described as a safe forced-shutdown action in this scenario.
explanation: The PDF's solution is to power off the VM from CAS to force shutdown without incurring problems; it does NOT recommend downgrading libvirtd or replacing the NIC type. The other statements match the documented condition and solution.

question: Concerning the resolution of 'failure to format the disk of a shared file system' and 'failure to set up the network with CAStools after deploying a Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 VM', which statements are INCORRECT? (Select all that apply.)
options: Disk formatting of a shared file system is available only when the storage pool has been specified for at least one host and the pool is not active.

If formatting fails because the pool is not attached, add a host pool that uses the shared file system to the host and suspend the storage pool, then reformat from the host pool / shared-file-system tab.
For the RHEL 7 CAStools network-setup failure, install the most recent version of CAStools on the VM and use 'chkconfig NetworkManager off' to disable NetworkManager, then reconfigure the template.
The shared-file-system disk can be formatted while the storage pool is active and currently mounted by running VMs.
The RHEL 7 CAStools issue is resolved by enabling NetworkManager via 'chkconfig NetworkManager on' and rebooting the host.
explanation: The PDF states the storage pool must NOT be active during reformat, and the RHEL 7 fix is to DISABLE NetworkManager ('chkconfig NetworkManager off') after updating CAStools, not enable it. The first three statements match the PDF's documented resolutions.
